content.text: Create React App (CRA) was the official React scaffolding tool from 2016 to 2023. It gave developers a zero-config webpack + Babel setup with Jest, ESLint, and CSS Modules pre-configured. While CRA is no longer the recommended starting point for new projects, it still powers a large number of existing codebases — understanding it is essential for working with legacy React applications.

attrs.alt: CRA webpack-based dev server vs Vite ESM-based dev server — showing bundle-first vs request-first architecture and performance differences
attrs.title: CRA vs Vite architecture comparison

content.text: The React team removed it from their official documentation in early 2023. The npm package still works, but it receives no security updates and its webpack version lags behind. New projects should use Vite or a framework like Next.js. Read this section to understand CRA projects you encounter, not to start new ones.
Before CRA, setting up a React project required manually configuring webpack, Babel, and a dozen plugins. CRA hid all of that inside a single
package, so developers could start coding immediately.
Bundled all modules (JS, CSS, images) into one or a few files
Transpiled JSX and modern JS to browser-compatible code
Unit testing with jsdom environment, pre-configured
Linting with react-app ruleset, runs on every build
Import SVGs as React components via webpack loader

# JavaScript (original)
npx create-react-app my-app

# TypeScript template
npx create-react-app my-app --template typescript

cd my-app
npm start
. On the first run webpack bundles every module in the project — this is why cold starts take 10–60 seconds on non-trivial projects.

my-app/
├── public/
│   ├── index.html          # HTML shell — CRA injects bundle here
│   ├── favicon.ico
│   └── manifest.json       # PWA manifest
├── src/
│   ├── App.tsx             # root component
│   ├── App.css
│   ├── App.test.tsx        # Jest test for App
│   ├── index.tsx           # entry — ReactDOM.render / createRoot
│   ├── index.css
│   ├── logo.svg
│   └── react-app-env.d.ts  # CRA's ambient type declarations
└── package.json
visible in the project root. The
package owns all of that configuration internally.
Start webpack dev server with HMR on port 3000
Run Jest in interactive watch mode
Copy all config files to the project root (irreversible)
CRA also provides two special built-in variables without any prefix:
CRA's webpack configuration shipped with a built-in loader for importing SVGs as React components — a pattern that doesn't exist in standard Vite without a plugin:
copies all of the hidden webpack, Babel, ESLint, and Jest configuration into the project root, making it editable. This is the only official way to customise CRA beyond what
there is no un-eject. All configuration is now in your repo and you own all future upgrades.
hundreds of lines of webpack config and a dozen new npm dependencies appear in
If you need custom configuration, migrate to Vite instead of ejecting. A Vite project gives you full control without the overhead.
Before Vite's rise, teams used the community package
to patch webpack config without ejecting:
You may encounter this pattern in older CRA codebases. It works by monkey-patching the internal
webpack config at runtime. Fragile and not recommended for new work.
Migrating a CRA Project to Vite
If you inherit a CRA codebase, migration to Vite is straightforward for most projects and eliminates slow dev-server starts immediately.
Install Vite and the React plugin:

npm install --save-dev vite @vitejs/plugin-react
npm uninstall react-scripts
Create vite.config.ts at the project root:
Move index.html from public/ to the project root
placeholder with an empty string. Add the script tag:
Replace REACT_APP_ env vars with VITE_:
(optional but recommended for consistency with the Vite setup).
Reading CRA Code: Key Patterns to Recognise
When reading an existing CRA project, these patterns distinguish it from a Vite project:
webpack must bundle everything before the dev server is ready. This is architectural — no config change fixes it without switching tools. Migrate to Vite.
CRA pins ESLint at v7 internally. Installing ESLint v8+ globally or in devDependencies causes peer dependency warnings and can break
CRA's webpack 4 uses a legacy OpenSSL hash. Workaround:
. The real fix is migrating to Vite (webpack 5 or 5+ doesn't have this issue).
CRA ships with Jest and React Testing Library pre-installed and pre-configured. Test files are found by convention:

npm test             # interactive watch mode (default)
npm test -- --watchAll=false  # single run (CI)
npm test -- --coverage        # with coverage report
matchers, and CSS/image mocking automatically. No separate jest.config.js is needed in a non-ejected project.
CSS and Asset Handling in CRA
file imported in a component applies its rules globally. This is different from Vite's default — CRA does not scope regular CSS files.
CRA supports Sass without extra config — just install the compiler:

npm install sass
# Then rename .css to .scss and update imports
Files smaller than 10 KB are inlined as Base64 data URIs automatically. Larger files are copied to
with content-hashed filenames. The build includes:
— the HTML shell with injected script/link tags.
The build also prints a size analysis table to the terminal showing each chunk's gzipped size and whether it exceeds a 244 KB warning threshold.
CRA's dev server can proxy API requests to a backend server, avoiding CORS issues during development. Add a
All requests to an unknown path (not a static file) are forwarded to
. For more complex proxying (multiple targets, path rewrites), use
If you must maintain a CRA project without migrating, these practices reduce friction:
CRA's internal dependencies are tightly coupled. Use an exact version in
Unless the project will never be migrated to Vite, avoid ejecting. It trades a future clean migration for immediate config access.
Older CRA versions have fewer Node compatibility issues on Node 16. Node 18+ introduces the OpenSSL breaking change.
CRA generates source maps in production builds by default, doubling build time and outputting large
files. Disable unless you have a remote error tracker.
CRA supports absolute imports from the
from the config and instructs webpack to resolve bare specifiers relative to
. Unlike Vite, CRA does not support custom path aliases beyond
CRA can serve the dev server over HTTPS — useful when testing OAuth flows or browser APIs that require a secure origin:

# .env.local
HTTPS=true

# or as a one-time flag:
HTTPS=true npm start

CRA generates a self-signed certificate the first time HTTPS mode is used. The browser shows a security warning for self-signed certs — click through to proceed in development. For a trusted cert, use
to generate a locally trusted certificate and set
CRA used to generate a service worker by default using Workbox. As of CRA 4, the service worker is opt-in. The scaffold generates
When registered, CRA's Workbox service worker caches the entire app shell for offline use and serves stale content while revalidating in the background. Changing to
converts your CRA app into a fully offline-capable PWA. This feature exists in the
CRA runs ESLint as part of webpack's compilation pipeline — lint errors appear in the browser overlay and the terminal simultaneously. The ruleset
To customise rules without ejecting, add an
Note: you cannot change the ESLint parser or install ESLint plugins not already bundled with
without ejecting. That limitation is a common pain point and a frequent reason teams migrate to Vite.
for code splitting, producing separate chunks in the
component as a separate chunk file. The browser downloads it only when the user navigates to
. This behaviour is identical between CRA and Vite.
folder is a self-contained static site. Any static hosting works — serve the folder from the same deployment target as your API or use a CDN.
npm run build && npx gh-pages -d build
so CRA injects the correct asset prefix. The equivalent in Vite is
Key Environment Variables Built Into CRA
Start dev server on port 4000 instead of 3000
Serve dev server over HTTPS with self-signed cert
Skip source map generation in production build (faster, smaller)
Disable inline ESLint linting during webpack build
Prevent CRA from auto-opening a browser tab on start
Allow the build to succeed even if TypeScript has type errors (not recommended)
scaffold, Babel strips TypeScript type annotations before bundling (it does not type-check). A separate
pass runs as a parallel fork-ts-checker-webpack-plugin process and reports type errors in the terminal without blocking the bundle.
This means you can write a TypeScript error, save, and CRA's dev server still reloads — the type error shows in the terminal but does not block HMR. In Vite,
before Rollup, so type errors block the production build (which is safer). CRA's production build does the same — type errors abort
This pulls in CRA's ambient type declarations: SVG imports return
. Do not delete this file — TypeScript will produce errors for every non-JS import.
Alternative Scaffolding Tools Used Alongside CRA
During CRA's peak years, several other tools were used for React projects that CRA didn't suit:
Active — now the React team's primary recommendation
Static site generation, GraphQL data layer
Active but less dominant than Next.js
Active — current community default for SPAs
CRA intentionally hides its webpack config to keep things simple. When you need to extend it without ejecting, two libraries are widely used:
Merge extra webpack options via config-overrides.js
Full config layer — plugins for Tailwind, aliases, Babel presets
CRA displays a full-screen error overlay in the browser whenever the development build throws an unhandled error or a compile error occurs. Understanding the overlay speeds up debugging:
— compile error (TypeScript or syntax). Fix the file and save; the overlay disappears automatically when webpack re-compiles successfully.
— runtime error. Click a frame to jump directly to the source line in VS Code (requires the
env var set to your editor).
— hides the overlay temporarily so you can inspect the partially-rendered app. It reappears on the next re-render.
— CRA ships development source maps by default, so the overlay shows original TypeScript/JSX lines, not compiled JS.
CRA's webpack config produces a single HTML entry point. To serve multiple HTML pages (e.g. a marketing page and an app page), you need to either eject or use react-app-rewired:
For most new projects, Vite or Next.js are better choices for multi-page scenarios — this pattern is documented here only for maintaining legacy CRA apps.

CRA introduced zero-config React development in 2016 and served the community well, but its webpack-based architecture cannot match Vite's sub-second cold starts and module-level HMR. The tool is now unmaintained. For existing CRA projects: understand the
patterns, avoid ejecting, and plan a migration to Vite when the project allows.
